verses.text: The Jews ask Festus to bring Paul to Jerusalem, lying in wait to kill him. Old plot renewed. Their malice has not cooled with time.
The Jews ask Festus to bring Paul to Jerusalem, lying in wait to kill him. Old plot renewed. Their malice has not cooled with time.
The Jews ask Festus to bring Paul to Jerusalem, lying in wait to kill him. Old plot renewed. Their malice has not cooled with time.
Festus says they should come to Caesarea to accuse him. Fair refusal. He will not deliver Paul to their snare.
Festus says they should come to Caesarea to accuse him. Fair refusal. He will not deliver Paul to their snare.
They lay grievous complaints they cannot prove; Paul answers, I have offended nothing. Empty charges. The accusers have no evidence.
They lay grievous complaints they cannot prove; Paul answers, I have offended nothing. Empty charges. The accusers have no evidence.
They lay grievous complaints they cannot prove; Paul answers, I have offended nothing. Empty charges. The accusers have no evidence.
Festus, to please the Jews, asks if he will go up; Paul appeals 'unto Caesar.' A Roman's right. He turns to the emperor for justice.
Festus, to please the Jews, asks if he will go up; Paul appeals 'unto Caesar.' A Roman's right. He turns to the emperor for justice.
Festus, to please the Jews, asks if he will go up; Paul appeals 'unto Caesar.' A Roman's right. He turns to the emperor for justice.
Festus, to please the Jews, asks if he will go up; Paul appeals 'unto Caesar.' A Roman's right. He turns to the emperor for justice.
King Agrippa comes; Festus tells him of Paul. The case rehearsed. The matter is laid before the king.
King Agrippa comes; Festus tells him of Paul. The case rehearsed. The matter is laid before the king.
King Agrippa comes; Festus tells him of Paul. The case rehearsed. The matter is laid before the king.
King Agrippa comes; Festus tells him of Paul. The case rehearsed. The matter is laid before the king.
The accusers had no crime, but 'questions... of their own superstition, and of one Jesus,' whom Paul says is alive. Puzzled governor. He cannot grasp the real dispute.
The accusers had no crime, but 'questions... of their own superstition, and of one Jesus,' whom Paul says is alive. Puzzled governor. He cannot grasp the real dispute.
The accusers had no crime, but 'questions... of their own superstition, and of one Jesus,' whom Paul says is alive. Puzzled governor. He cannot grasp the real dispute.
Agrippa would hear him; 'Tomorrow... thou shalt hear him.' Curiosity. The king desires to see the famous prisoner.
Agrippa would hear him; 'Tomorrow... thou shalt hear him.' Curiosity. The king desires to see the famous prisoner.
Agrippa would hear him; 'Tomorrow... thou shalt hear him.' Curiosity. The king desires to see the famous prisoner.
Agrippa comes with pomp; Festus owns he has nothing certain to write. No charge. The Roman finds the prisoner blameless.
